transcript.transcript: Today is November 13th, Monday. This is an interview with Jim Hall in Boise, Idaho. Jim is the current Director of Parks and Recreation. And the year is 2006. Jim, I want to thank you for agreeing to talk to us today. This is for a project for the centennial of Julia Davis Park. And basically, I wanted to get some of your memories of this park, both as a professional and as a person. Great. Thanks, Marilyn. Well, I can tell you the very first time that I visited the park, and that was in 1968. I was in Boise for the state high school track meet, and I was running the next day, the mile relay, and Coach Ed Troxell's daughter, I was going out with her, was here and she invited me to go with her and drive around town and look at things. So we did, went to the park, the very last thing, and ended up Getting back a little bit late. That's how I broke curfew for the track team. I didn't get in trouble But so that's the first time I went to the park. It was very nice. We walked through the park and she gave me a bunch of history about it and it was rather intriguing. I thought it was something that was very very nice for the community. So and then the next time was in 1991 when I was hired to be director of Boise Parks and Recreation and getting to know all what 35 parks at that time was fun. Learned a little bit of history. The challenging thing about Julia Davis Park though, there had never been a master plan done for the park. We knew about the 100 year anniversary coming up in 2007. We had the original gift documents, but other than having any type of history, there really was none. Particularly a lot of history about who Julia Davis was, Tom Davis, when he tried to give the park to the city, the fact that a number of the City Council members didn't want it. They thought it was just a bog and they didn't feel it was something that they wanted to do and I think it took several years for the City Council actually accepted it as a park. The interesting thing is we didn't find out really a lot of the history until we started putting together the master plan in 1995 and I felt as though before we did any other improvements to the park long term, for example, the expansion of the zoo, what do we do with the roadways, what do we do with new shelters, restrooms, how do we deal with the historical perspective. We needed a master plan. One of the ironic things was that we had pretty much as a city lost contact with the family for a number of years and I think when Tom Davis left and then his children came on there really wasn't, I don't think at that point in time, a good feeling about how the city may have treated them, particularly as time went on. There didn't appear to be that opportunity. So when we started the master plan, we were able to find Dianne Davis-Myklegard, and through working with her very early on probably probably 1994 we began the dialogue she introduced me to her father Tom Davis introduced me to a number of family members when they had the family reunion and they gave me two t-shirts that they were going to wear in the Fourth of July parade at Cascade. Every family member had one of the t-shirts and it's actually the Julia Davis Park emblem or picture that sets on top of the master plan which signifies really the history and the activities and some of those items. So that was fun. We got we got the opportunity to meet with Diane. She was instrumental in the master plan. Got to meet the family, they were instrumental in developing the master plan. Then they threw this gala party down at Julia Davis Park and I had the opportunity to go as their guest for the reunion and it was very fun. I missed the Fourth of July parade though. I had something else going on. I was invited to go up and walk that as well. Since that time, we've worked pretty closely with Diane and her father. And one of the more significant things that we did, we looked at the new arch going, having a new gateway entry sign. The one that we have put in was somewhat the same one that was originally put in the park and that would have been like River Street entrance. The entrance was actually more down where the alley is in the park, which is that long linear sidewalk that goes basically from the art museum to the zoo. That apparently at one point in time was part of River Street. And there was an arch over that street as you went into the park. Nobody could find it anymore. There were places where people thought it might have been, but they would never locate it. So what happened was Winco, when they were building their new store, came to our department and asked if they could put storm water from their roof and their parking lot into the park rather than dumping it into the storm drains And we told them that that is something that is possible. And the requirements were that they had to pre-treat it, and to make sure they took out all the oil and grease. They had to address what would happen if there was any type of hazardous spill that would go into the park and they were liable for that. Taking the stormwater was is actually good because it has the opportunity to somewhat filter out before it goes back into the river where the wetlands is by the zoo and we're going to extend that. So what we did is we told Winco that there was a price involved in taking their water and they said, well, how much would it be? And we told them, how does $50, 000 sound? And we would design a new gateway entry going into the park. And they said, great. And we did a design, gave it to them and rather than have the city accept the money and build it, it would have been much more expensive for us. Winco just handled the entire project And they were very cordial. We did a dedication with parts of it. Was that about two years ago? No, when was it? No, it's longer now. It had to be somewhere around 2000, 2001. Probably around 2000 because it's been there at least six years. And then what we did next was we took some additional stormwater from the Edith County Courthouse and a couple other businesses and I think that netted somewhere around oh ten thousand And then we worked with Mr. Davis who chipped in, I think, ten, and we were able to do the memorial for Julia Davis. And that was the other thing. There wasn't even a sign in the park telling folks who Julia Davis was. So the art commission assisted in working with prospective artists. One was selected with the Arts Commission and our staff. They designed the memorial pretty much after meeting with the family members. They got her actual size, that is her, which is, she was a very small woman and they also from the historical research with the family and also I believe some of the archives found that she obviously would greet folks that were coming through town on the old, on the Oregon Trail, because it passed right through there on a ferry. And she would hand out fruit like apples because part of the property was actually an orchard, an apple orchard. So she would hand out apples to the folks coming in and that's where she apparently contracted her illness and then died. So the family wanted to try to depict how she may have looked. They actually had some pictures of her, how she may have looked with her size, the type of clothes, the clothing of the era, and then what the message would be. So we designed, we designed the memorial and the city, through the Arts Commission, our staff hired an artist who created a bronze sculpture. So, let's see... See I think that's in respect to some of the more major items that went on at the park we've obviously done a bunch of repairs over the time since I've been here. For example, the band show. The band show was named after Gene Harris, working with the family. We liked that. We also allowed for the relocation of the former church, Baptist Church, which became the Black History Museum. We worked extensively with them, the Black History group. And that was probably one of the more fun projects I think I've had the opportunity to do because it really was important I think to look at diversity in our culture. So those are probably the major items and then we moved after that from at least 2000 if not 1999. That's when Diane started talking about the hundred-year anniversary and what we could do and we were looking at how we could fund that. Unfortunately, the last three years were kind of leaning on money, so we couldn't really implement a lot other than major repairs and we're going to put some significant money into that this next year with roadway improvements and of course the partnership with BSU on the tennis courts which is a $600, 000 project that's underway right now. Will they be at the same place? Yeah they are, we tore them out and we put about $500, 000 into the repairs, just basically redoing them, into a new design which is hundred thousand dollars into the repairs I'm just basically redoing a new design which is post tension concrete rather than just asphalt. Asphalt doesn't have the durability maybe 15 to 20 years max. Concrete will last virtually, there's courts that have been built in the 60s that are still doing well in concrete. And so we, I worked with Gene Blamar who wanted to be able to use those courts for intercollegiate play. So we worked out a deal where he would come up with 77, 000, that's what we needed, 577, 000 to get the courts done. And then he would go out and get additional funding to pay basically the athletic department back to you and he would as long as you got the money we were going to allow him to do naming rights on the courts. So he was able to come up with the money to not only repay his account for the tennis courts, but we also got a $25, 000 donation from Micron to do one in the court. So we came up with all the money we needed to redo the courts. Good. Do you have any thoughts about the park as it relates to other urban areas or the role it plays? Well, I think any time you have a park of that size, basically in the core of the city it becomes really a magnet for a lot of good activities. The same thing with Ann Morrison, Calvin Elvis and Albertson is just a little bit different, it's a specialty kind of park. Ann Morrison and Julie Davis have similar roles in that they can both accommodate large events up to a degree. Julie Davis can can hold large events, Ann Morrison obviously more. So when you have parts of that style, what you're able to do is put activities that can actually draw people to the downtown area from all over to take part in whether it be gathering as a place to march to the Capitol, Art in the Park, or all the other events that go on there, from family reunions to concerts that the band shall. It provides a venue that just makes the downtown much more habitable and a nice place to be. I think that's one of the reasons you're starting to see more downtown housing development because the park is one of the major amenities that people can have as they move into the downtown area. And it continues to play that role. What is it, the 48th year, something like that of park to park, which is significant in itself. I think the other reason it plays such a significant role is it really has evolved into more of a cultural facility with the art Museum, the State Historical Museum, Black History Museum, the zoo, and the Discovery Center, which potentially will leave once they get their own facility. So it plays a role as well in respect of education in the community and the surrounding area and visitors. What is your, The Discovery Center is on Julie Davis property. What is the master plan called for? Well, the master plan for all of those facilities allowed a certain amount of development to occur, increasing the size of the building. However, what we had to do as a part of allowing them to increase the size, we had to keep a no net loss of green space in the park. So if we build a square foot of concrete or building we have to come up with another square foot of grass. So we did the master plan shows how that expansion can take place by relocating the road along the river taking that road out all together turning that into just a pedestrian only greenbelt, so that saved a lot of square footage in respect to taking out that could go into buildings. Plus we're centralizing the parking around all the buildings rather than along the river which has a Tennessee service VSU for parking because they have a lack of it. That's what we've also put a two hour time limit on the parking at Julia Davis so it's available for the customer rather than VSU parking. Are there any particular challenges that you faced with the park? Well, other than we want to make sure this event for 2007 is really a signature event, not only from the standpoint that it recognizes the importance of the park and the role it plays in the community, but also the fact that it's really due for a facelift and the addition of components that we feel and the citizens group that evaluated architectural competition that occurred last fall in the spring will help make the park even that more greater as an asset to the city. And will those, the winners of the competition for like including the restrooms and adding various things, will that be funded partly by the city? What we're hopeful is that a majority of those new items would be funded through donations and then what the city would do, would do all the major repair items like fix all the roadways, take the roadway out along the river, redo the restrooms, the current restrooms and the shelters and then as a part of fundraising they would look at the three central locations within the park for the new shelters, the formal seating areas, the entries, so there's a lot of... And the new restrooms, so they would have a much greater role in actually taking the park to this new look and we would be taking the park to fixing everything that needs to be fixed and nobody's going to get money to go out and fix a road but they will for something that's very attractive. Those are really the only challenges. It's a park that received very little vandalism. It receives one heck of a lot of use and the use demand is outpacing our ability to provide the use to everyone. So it's got something pretty much in there all the time, whether it be large events or picnics, reservations, fundraising activities for some group that wants to host a pancake feed like the Kiwanis used to do on the 4th of July. It's hard to squeeze any more activities in, particularly activities that are large. Particularly at teams that are large. What would you say is, can you give me some examples of anything very unique that you can recall over the years that's happened there. I know that, for example, I don't know that there's some nights in the summer, there's hockey sack players that have competitions. I mean, some of those kinds of informal. I'm sure there's a lot of activities that could be on or off the record. There's... Our employees have seen some pretty outrageous activities going on. It has been a place for a lot of informal activities like hacky sack or the drummers that would come there and drum every Wednesday or Thursday night. They had a lot of fun doing that. They weren't annoying. Most people were in other locations of the park. Besides that, it sounded kind of nice. There were probably some challenges, the police could tell you, with some illicit drug activities, but very, very little. For the most part, they were, they were pretty good. See, what other things? There's been some baptism, you know, baptism sites. Right. Baptism, you know, baptism. Right. In the river. Right. Weddings, memorial services, it pretty much plays the role of a lot of different activities. Some religious events which are fun. There was a group that would, every Saturday I think, they were feeding homeless at the park. I think they've no longer doing that. Other service groups are picking that up and it is a consortium of religious groups in the community we're doing it. Let's see... Ways to stay football players during practice Oh, I'm sure they practice I think the yeah, I think the I think the tailgate parties are something that hasn't really harmed the park. There's not a lot either in the park and the Park Commission wouldn't allow that to occur. There's going to be some spontaneous ones but they're not going to be able to be full-fledged site. Matter of fact we were asked by BSU this year if we would allow all their tailgaters because they're going to change some of their policies and be a little bit more strict on consumption of alcohol. So they want to know if it was okay if they went over the park and you said no, no thank you. And one thing we did do, we did have some problems with, I guess you could call them some of the transients, some of the folks that really didn't have much to do during the day, and tubers which would drink all along the Greenbelt within very short distance of the Greenbelt. They were just, they were disturbing patrons walking along the Greenbelt or Picnickers or whoever it may be so we took alcohol out within 250 feet of the Greenbelt and in essence We already had part of Julia Davis alcohol-free zone if you if you take into account where the Rose Garden and the Banshee and of course the zoo. So we just, while we were at it, we just took the rest of the park and put it within the 250 feet. There was a spot before we did the entire park, it was up by the Discovery Center, and the folks that were drinking, it didn't take them long to figure out where that line was so they went above that line and then we moved the line up and now there's no place to drink. And it's been a lot better. So the whole park is alcohol free now? Yeah, except by permit. And then that pretty much took care of that problem and I think we've received a lot of very, a lot of good compliments for doing that. It's just made the park a lot more friendly and particularly along Greenville it's eliminated a lot of the bad activities and then the City Council of course took alcohol off the river, which I'd proposed probably 1996 and the Mayor and Council at the time didn't really want to tackle it until the current mayor and council got in and actually saw all the problems and the police department and Parson Wright had been trying to do that for some time and that's just made it so much more enjoyable. It's now back to a family event And the bad activities that were occurring along the river diminished significantly. Well, I think the Rose Garden is, there's some history there, that the Rose Garden's been there for a very long time, has been a signature location and during the master plan there was quite a bit of discussion about whether or not the Rose Garden could be moved to the east side of the park rather than... So there could be a more passive area at that end of the park because East End, West End is the most heavily used area within the park and in a way some of the noisier activities really didn't go with the Rose Garden from a contemplating view by some. So I met with the Rose Society one day and telling them that that was kind of an idea that the consultant had come up with. Consultant had come up with. And these are fairly, at the time they were fairly older women who were either retired or wanting to have some hobbies so they would go in there and work on the roses calling they were going to deadhead all the roses cut the old buds off so we we worked with them for years so when that proposal came up I'm lucky I made it out of the meeting alive. And they were sweet. I was this 45-year-old guy in there, and they were probably all well into their 70s. And so they used their motherly approach on me and and it was pretty evident that would never happen And then what we did about the same time to make amends with them. I mean they weren't angry they were just they were...

lines.line: # Introduction

> The interview begins with an introduction of the interviewee, Jim Hall, Director of Parks and Recreation, and the purpose of the interview: to gather memories for the centennial of Julia Davis Park.
** introduction, Jim Hall, Parks and Recreation, centennial, Julia Davis Park **

lines.line: # First Visit to Julia Davis Park
> Jim Hall recounts his initial visit to Julia Davis Park in 1968, during a state high school track meet in Boise, which led to his first impression of the park.
** first visit, Julia Davis Park, 1968, track meet **
keywords: ** first visit, Julia Davis Park, 1968, track meet **

metadata.title: First Visit to Julia Davis Park

metadata.keywords: first visit, Julia Davis Park, 1968, track meet

lines.line: # Becoming Director

> Jim reflects on becoming the Director of Boise Parks and Recreation in 1991. He discusses the challenges faced due to the absence of a master plan for the park, especially in light of its upcoming 100-year anniversary.
** Director, Boise Parks and Recreation, 1991, master plan **
keywords: ** Director, Boise Parks and Recreation, 1991, master plan **

metadata.title: Becoming Director
metadata.synopsis: Jim reflects on becoming the Director of Boise Parks and Recreation in 1991. He discusses the challenges faced due to the absence of a master plan for the park, especially in light of its upcoming 100-year anniversary.
metadata.keywords: Director, Boise Parks and Recreation, 1991, master plan

lines.line: # History and the Master Plan
> Jim reveals the lack of historical records regarding Julia Davis and the initial resistance by the City Council to accept the park. A master plan begun in 1995 helped uncover much of this history.
** history, Julia Davis, City Council, master plan, 1995 **
keywords: ** history, Julia Davis, City Council, master plan, 1995 **

metadata.title: History and the Master Plan
metadata.synopsis: Jim reveals the lack of historical records regarding Julia Davis and the initial resistance by the City Council to accept the park. A master plan begun in 1995 helped uncover much of this history.
metadata.keywords: history, Julia Davis, City Council, master plan, 1995

lines.line: # Family Involvement and the Gala Party
> The interview covers the involvement of the Davis family in the park's master plan, culminating in a family reunion and gala party at Julia Davis Park.
** Davis family, involvement, gala party, reunion **
keywords: ** Davis family, involvement, gala party, reunion **
